EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water releases by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers from the Jamestown Reservoir and the Pipestem Reservoir continued to cause flooding on the James River at Columbia throughout June.  The added water also caused the James River at Stratford to rise to above flood stage on June 23rd in the early morning hours.  The James River at Columbia rose to nearly 15 feet or almost 2 feet above flood stage. Low-lying agricultural land along with a few roads were flooded along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing moderate to heavy ice accumulations, ranging up to more than a quarter of an inch. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 8.5 inches near Armour. Numerous branches and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind, with much of the power line damage affecting rural electric cooperatives. Trees and tree debris blocked roads, and damaged some vehicles and homes. Several power outages were reported. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain and freezing drizzle producing light ice accumulations, then changing to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching the 5 to 10 inch range. The winter precipitation made travel very difficult, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain and freezing drizzle producing light ice accumulations, then changing to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 10.5 inches near Winfred. The winter precipitation made travel very difficult, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing heavy ice accumulations, with a quarter to a half inch in the northern part of the county. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 9 inches in Marion. Many branches, limbs, whole trees, and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. Both urban areas and rural electric cooperatives suffered major power line damage. Trees and tree debris blocked many roads, and damaged several vehicles and homes. Major power outages were reported, affecting thousands of people. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

Flooding on the James River continued into August across Brown County.  The river fell below flood stage at Columbia on August 8th in the early afternoon but continued to flood at Stratford through August. Lowland flooding along with a few roads were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in January. Precipitation was below to well below normal, although with the low midwinter normals, even greater precipitation would have been unlikely to change the dry soil conditions. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was becoming more vulnerable to even marginally weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, with below to much below normal precipitation during the month bringing no relief. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to strong northwest winds and bitterly cold air combined to lower wind chill readings to 20 to 40 degrees below zero over part of southeast South Dakota from the evening of January 20th through the morning of January 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds 15 to 30 mph combined with temperatures well below zero to lower wind chills to 35 to 40 degrees below zero.

There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to exceptional for the area, with the northern edge of the area, generally from Huron to Brookings, making a slight improvement to severe because of greater snowfall and snow cover during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, despite above normal precipitation, due to the excess being only several tenths of an inch during the normally driest month. Although there was a very slight improvement in the outlook due to the above normal snowfall and snow cover, drought was still considered severe. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the sensitivity of the spring and summer to dry weather if it developed, and which could negate the slight improvement in the outlook.

Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was becoming more vulnerable to even marginally weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, with below to much below normal precipitation during the month bringing no relief. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow mixed became all snow during the daytime hours of March 9th in the area from Huron southwest to Chamberlain and Gregory. The wet snow continued into the evening with accumulations of 3 to 6 inches. There was some minor drifting as northerly winds averaged 15 to 25 mph. There were gusts to 30 mph near the end of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to wet snow and accumulated 3 to 5 inches. Huron reported 4.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense and slow moving spring snow storm impacted the area for over 48 hours. This storm brought heavy snowfall to much of the area, especially from the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 12 to 24 inches were reported across much of the Black Hills and south central South Dakota plains, with some locations receiving around 30 inches. The Rapid City Regional Airport received a record 28.2 inches of snow, including a record 20 inches in one day. To the north and east of the Black Hills, six to 18 inches of snow fell. Snowfall tapered off near the North Dakota border, where less than six inches of snow fell. Wind gusts to 40 mph produced considerable blowing and drifting snow on the plains. Interstate 90 was closed across much of western South Dakota for 24 hours.

A severe thunderstorm moved northeastward across Ziebach County and produced golf ball size hail in the Red Elm area. Three to five inches of rain fell in less than two hours, causing several small streams and creeks to quickly overflow their banks. Water flowed over roads and flooded pastures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over four inches of rain fell in two hours. Runoff washed gravel off several roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over four inches of rain fell in two hours, causing Little Cottonwood and Big Cottonwood Creeks to overflow their banks.

Thunderstorms developed along the eastern slopes of the Black Hills and adjacent plains from Nisland to Sturgis to the Rapid City area. As these storms moved toward the northwest, they redeveloped and became stationary over the eastern foothills. As much as six inches of rain fell in a few hours. Flash flooding was reported in Rapid City; Sturgis; and rural areas of Butte, Meade, and Pennington Counties. Runoff from the rainfall quickly inundated city streets, overflowed small creeks, and breached a few private stock dams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in a few hours and caused flash flooding in southeastern Butte County. Willow Creek, Horse Creek, Indian Creek, and Whitewood Creek quickly rose over their banks. At least two private stock dams failed southeast of Nisland. Numerous roads, culverts, and fences were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to six inches of rain caused flash flooding in the Rapid City area. Rapid Creek overflowed its banks and flooded portions of the bike path and several roads. A few structures along Rapid Creek were flooded near South Valley Drive.

Water releases by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers from the Jamestown Reservoir and the Pipestem Reservoir continued to cause flooding on the James River at Columbia and Stratford throughout July. The James River at Columbia crested at 15.39 feet or 2 feet above flood stage during the morning hours on the 10th. The James River near Stratford crested at 14.99 feet or 1 foot above flood stage during the mid-morning hours on the 25th.  Significant low-lying agricultural lands along with some county roads were impacted from the flooding.

Heavy rain fell over portions of the Rosebud Reservation. Rain runoff, debris flows from the 2012 Longhorn Fire Complex, and the failure of Omaha Boy Dam caused flash flooding in the Rosebud area of central Todd County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris clogged the outlet works at Ghost Hawk Dam and Rosebud Dam, causing the dams to overflow after heavy rain. In addition, a breach of Omaha Boy Dam, about a mile southwest of the Rosebud Dam, increased inflows into the lake. Several culverts and roads were damaged by the flooding, including Ghost Hawk Road and Grass Mountain Road. The Little White River near Rosebud rose from less than 100 cfs to over 1000 cfs in less than four hours, with a peak flow of 1300 cfs.

A line of storms moving across north central South Dakota during the early morning hours on the 14th became heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms for northeastern South Dakota.  Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches caused mainly localized flooding.  However in Grant County, a 20 foot wide by 17 foot deep culvert on 468th Avenue in Twin Brooks was washed away by flash flooding. The Grant County Highway Department said fixing the culvert would cost more than $40,000 dollars.

A pair of severe storms moved across northeastern South Dakota during the evening hours of the 9th.  These storms caused extensive damage to crops, especially west of Frederick in Brown County where beans and corn fields were completely destroyed.  As the storms moved from Barnard, through Columbia and into the Groton area, the hail increased to baseball size. There was also some structural damage to siding along with broken windows.

A strong warm front along with very unstable air and strong deep layer winds brought several supercell thunderstorns along with a damaging line of thunderstorms/bow echo to parts of central and northeast South Dakota during the afternoon hours.  Damaging winds up to 90 mph uprooted large trees and caused considerable structural and crop damage and loss of power to those in it's path. The worst wind damage was located at Lake Poinsett, Watertown, and Milbank.  A woman was killed and her husband was seriously injured on Lake Poinsett when their lake house was destroyed. Numerous trees were downed along with many structures damaged or destroyed. Many trees had fallen onto homes, cabins, and trailers. The bowling alley in Clear Lake lost its roof along with numerous pole barns being destroyed along the storms path. Thousands of people were also left without power. Four tornado touchdowns occurred along with hail up to the size of softballs. Isolated flash flooding also occurred.  Codington, Hamlin, Grant, and Deuel counties were all declared in a Federal Disaster Declaration. Total damage estimates were around 1,100,000 dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail broke many windows in their house as hail lasted nearly thirty minutes with 2 to 3 inches of hail accumulating on the ground.

A long-lived severe thunderstorm developed over the southern Black Hills and moved eastward across the South Dakota plains during the morning. The storm produced very large hail to softball size from eastern Custer to northern Jackson Counties.
A storm briefly became severe over Fall River County and produced golf ball size hail near Provo.

A strong warm front along with very unstable air and strong deep layer winds brought several supercell thunderstorns along with a damaging line of thunderstorms/bow echo to parts of central and northeast South Dakota during the afternoon hours.  Damaging winds up to 90 mph uprooted large trees and caused considerable structural and crop damage and loss of power to those in it's path. The worst wind damage was located at Lake Poinsett, Watertown, and Milbank.  A woman was killed and her husband was seriously injured on Lake Poinsett when their lake house was destroyed. Numerous trees were downed along with many structures damaged or destroyed. Many trees had fallen onto homes, cabins, and trailers. The bowling alley in Clear Lake lost its roof along with numerous pole barns being destroyed along the storms path. Thousands of people were also left without power. Four tornado touchdowns occurred along with hail up to the size of softballs. Isolated flash flooding also occurred.  Codington, Hamlin, Grant, and Deuel counties were all declared in a Federal Disaster Declaration. Total damage estimates were around 1,100,000 dollars.

The worst wind damage was located at Lake Poinsett, Watertown, and Milbank.  A woman was killed and her husband was seriously injured on Lake Poinsett when their lake house was destroyed. Numerous trees were downed along with many structures damaged or destroyed. Many trees had fallen onto homes, cabins, and trailers. The bowling alley in Clear Lake lost its roof along with numerous pole barns being destroyed along the storms path. Thousands of people were also left without power. Four tornado touchdowns occurred along with hail up to the size of softballs. Isolated flash flooding also occurred.  Codington, Hamlin, Grant, and Deuel counties were all declared in a Federal Disaster Declaration. Total damage estimates were around 1,100,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system pushed across the Northern Plains, bringing heavy rain to the northern Black Hills. Embedded thunderstorms produced additional heavy rain, with four to six inches of rain falling over a 24 hour period. Runoff caused minor flooding along the Belle Fourche River and its tributaries. Several county roads were closed or washed out and culverts were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redwater River, Crow Creek, and several small creeks and streams experienced flooding. Water reached the edge of a house along the Redwater River and some low water crossings were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over far northeastern Wyoming and tracked eastward across the northern Black Hills. The storms produced hail and gusty winds from the Spearfish area to Sturgis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large pine trees, spruce trees, and power poles were broken along the eastern rim of Spearfish Canyon. The worst damage occurred from Bridal Veil Falls to Victoria Lane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed several grain bins and damaged an attached two-stall garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front along with very unstable air and strong deep layer winds brought several supercell thunderstorns along with a damaging line of thunderstorms/bow echo to parts of central and northeast South Dakota during the afternoon hours.  Damaging winds up to 90 mph uprooted large trees and caused considerable structural and crop damage and loss of power to those in it's path. The worst wind damage was located at Lake Poinsett, Watertown, and Milbank.  A woman was killed and her husband was seriously injured on Lake Poinsett when their lake house was destroyed. Numerous trees were downed along with many structures damaged or destroyed. Many trees had fallen onto homes, cabins, and trailers. The bowling alley in Clear Lake lost its roof along with numerous pole barns being destroyed along the storms path. Thousands of people were also left without power. Four tornado touchdowns occurred along with hail up to the size of softballs. Isolated flash flooding also occurred.  Codington, Hamlin, Grant, and Deuel counties were all declared in a Federal Disaster Declaration. Total damage estimates were around 1,100,000 dollars.

Severe to exceptional drought conditions remained throughout January across central and northeast South Dakota.  Exceptional to extreme drought conditions covered much of central and north central South Dakota throughout January. An area of extreme drought conditions remained across east central South Dakota including the counties of Grant, Deuel, eastern Codington, and eastern Hamlin. Severe drought conditions covered much of the rest of the region except for Day, Marshall and northern Clark County.  On average, calculated soil moisture anomalies were showing deficits from 2 to 4 inches across the area.  Winter wheat and alfalfa were generally rated as poor to very poor for January.

Thunderstorms produced two tornadoes which tracked over extreme southeast Union County in southeast South Dakota on the late afternoon on October 4th. One tornado crossed over the Missouri River into Union County from Dakota County Nebraska. The other tornado formed in Union County and crossed the Big Sioux River into Plymouth County Iowa. Both tornadoes caused damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed the Missouri River from Dakota County in Nebraska and entered Union County. The tornado severely damaged the roof of a house, and collapsed a chimney, carport and porch roof, and the walls of a garage in the same house. The tornado also caused tree damage, including trunks snapped, overturned an irrigation system, and  damaged the roof, siding, and/or gutters of at least four other houses.

Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in January. Precipitation was below to well below normal, although with the low midwinter normals, even greater precipitation would have been unlikely to change the dry soil conditions. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was becoming more vulnerable to even marginally weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, with below to much below normal precipitation during the month bringing no relief. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

A strong winter storm moved across the Central Plains, bringing snow and gusty winds to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across southwestern South Dakota, where five to ten inches of snow was reported. Wind gusts around 40 mph combined with the snow to produce blowing and drifting snow, which reduced visibilities to below a quarter mile.

An upper level system moved across the Northern Plains, bringing snow to much of the area. Heavy upslope snow developed over the northern Black Hills during the evening of January 1 and persisted into the 2nd. The heaviest snow fell overnight, with around a foot of snow reported from Lead and Deadwood areas to Cheyenne Crossing. Localized accumulations around six inches also fell over eastern Shannon County and eastern Pennington County.
Strong southwesterly winds developed across the northern foothills of the Black Hills during the evening. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded near Crow Peak west of Spearfish.

Wind gusts around 40 mph combined with the snow to produce blowing and drifting snow, which reduced visibilities to below a quarter mile.

Severe to exceptional drought conditions remained throughout January across central and northeast South Dakota.  Exceptional to extreme drought conditions covered much of central and north central South Dakota throughout January. An area of extreme drought conditions remained across east central South Dakota including the counties of Grant, Deuel, eastern Codington, and eastern Hamlin. Severe drought conditions covered much of the rest of the region except for Day, Marshall and northern Clark County.  On average, calculated soil moisture anomalies were showing deficits from 2 to 4 inches across the area.  Winter wheat and alfalfa were generally rated as poor to very poor for January.

The severe to exceptional drought conditions continued across most of central and northeast South Dakota throughout February. A significant blizzard in early February brought from 1 to 2 feet of snow to northeast South Dakota into central South Dakota helping the drought situation. The counties in the northeast went from severe to extreme drought at the end of January to generally moderate drought by the mid week of February as a result of the blizzard.||The counties in exceptional drought were Stanley, Jones, and Lyman.  Extreme drought covered Dewey, Sully, Potter, Hughes, Buffalo, and southern Hand and Hyde counties. Walworth, Campbell, Corson, Mcpherson, Edmunds, and western Faulk remained in severe drought through the end of the month. Areas mainly west of the Missouri River did not receive as much moisture during February. In fact, north central South Dakota was generally below normal for precipitation. Calculated soil moisture deficits continued to range from 2 to 4 inches. Winter wheat continued to generally be rated from very poor to fair across the region.

Snow accumulated 6 to 10 inches during the night of February 21st and morning of February 22nd. The snowfall was accompanied by light to moderate winds. Classes at several schools were delayed or cancelled for February 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 9 inches. Travel was hampered and schools were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 10 inches during the night of February 21st and morning of February 22nd. The snowfall was accompanied by light to moderate winds. Classes at several schools were delayed or cancelled for February 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches, including 5.5 inches at Menno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Variable snowfall of 2 to 8 inches, northwest winds gusting to 45 mph, and snow cover existing before the storm in part of the area, produced blizzard conditions with visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow in many areas. The low visibilities and drifting snow forced some businesses to close, and also forced several school closings on Monday February 11th. The blizzard closed many roads, including Interstates 90 and 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches, including 6.5 inches near Alexandria, was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to 45 mph, producing blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile. The low visibilities and drifting snow closed roads including Interstate 90, some businesses, and forced school closings on Monday February 11th.

Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in February, despite precipitation which was a little above normal. The excess of a few tenths of an inch in the driest month of the year did little to relieve the long term dry soil conditions. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the poor germination of the winter wheat crop during the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few during the winter because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to exceptional for the area, with the northern edge of the area, generally from Huron to Brookings, making a slight improvement to severe because of greater snowfall and snow cover during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued, despite above normal precipitation, due to the excess being only a few tenths of an inch during the normally driest month. Few new effects were noted, with the seasonally low water usage. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure area moving across the region brought widespread heavy snow of 6 to as much as 19 inches. Along with the heavy snow came very strong winds of 30 to 50 mph causing widespread blowing and drifting snow. Roads, highways, along with Interstates 29 and 90 were closed for a time. Schools started late or were closed on Monday the 11th.  A man died from exposure when he left his vehicle after he became stranded about three miles west of Redfield.  Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Murdo; 7 inches at Kennebec and Onida; 8 inches east of Hayes and Bowdle; 9 inches at Roscoe; 10 inches at Watertown, Miller, Clear Lake, Doland, and Highmore; and 11 inches at Castlewood.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Clark and Ipswich; 13 inches at Columbia and Aberdeen; 14 inches at Milbank and Faulkton; 15 inches at Waubay; 16 inches at Britton and Victor; 17 inches at Sisseton, Summit, and Wilmot; 18 inches at Webster; and 19 inches three miles west of Sisseton. The highest wind gust was 48 mph at Pierre during the late afternoon of the 10th. The snow began between 8 pm and Midnight on the 9th and ended in the late afternon of the 10th across central South Dakota and around noon on the 11th across the northeast.

The low visibilities and drifting snow forced some businesses to close, and also forced several school closings on Monday February 11th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to 45 mph, producing blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile. The low visibilities and drifting snow closed roads, some businesses, and forced school closings on Monday February 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure area moving across the region brought widespread heavy snow of 6 to as much as 19 inches. Along with the heavy snow came very strong winds of 30 to 50 mph causing widespread blowing and drifting snow. Roads, highways, along with Interstates 29 and 90 were closed for a time. Schools started late or were closed on Monday the 11th.  A man died from exposure when he left his vehicle after he became stranded about three miles west of Redfield.  Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Murdo; 7 inches at Kennebec and Onida; 8 inches east of Hayes and Bowdle; 9 inches at Roscoe; 10 inches at Watertown, Miller, Clear Lake, Doland, and Highmore; and 11 inches at Castlewood.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Clark and Ipswich; 13 inches at Columbia and Aberdeen; 14 inches at Milbank and Faulkton; 15 inches at Waubay; 16 inches at Britton and Victor; 17 inches at Sisseton, Summit, and Wilmot; 18 inches at Webster; and 19 inches three miles west of Sisseton. The highest wind gust was 48 mph at Pierre during the late afternoon of the 10th. The snow began between 8 pm and Midnight on the 9th and ended in the late afternon of the 10th across central South Dakota and around noon on the 11th across the northeast.

The severe to exceptional drought conditions continued across most of central and northeast South Dakota throughout February. A significant blizzard in early February brought from 1 to 2 feet of snow to northeast South Dakota into central South Dakota helping the drought situation. The counties in the northeast went from severe to extreme drought at the end of January to generally moderate drought by the mid week of February as a result of the blizzard.||The counties in exceptional drought were Stanley, Jones, and Lyman.  Extreme drought covered Dewey, Sully, Potter, Hughes, Buffalo, and southern Hand and Hyde counties. Walworth, Campbell, Corson, Mcpherson, Edmunds, and western Faulk remained in severe drought through the end of the month. Areas mainly west of the Missouri River did not receive as much moisture during February. In fact, north central South Dakota was generally below normal for precipitation. Calculated soil moisture deficits continued to range from 2 to 4 inches. Winter wheat continued to generally be rated from very poor to fair across the region.

An Arctic front dropping in behind an exiting low pressure area brought some scattered light snow showers, very strong winds of 30 to 50 mph, along with widespread blowing snow.  With the deep existing snow cover along with the new snowfall, ground blizzard conditions occurred from the late morning to the evening across much of northeast South Dakota.  Additional amounts from a trace up to an inch occurred. Frequent whiteout conditions brought extremely hazardous travel, with travel not advised along with several roads closed. Many mortorists were stranded and had to be rescued. Several schools were closed early and opened late on Tuesday the 19th. The highest wind gust was 47 mph at Hayti and Summit.  In part due to the low visibility and blowing snow, a van and a semi collided on U.S. Highway 281 two miles south of Warner around 11 am on the 18th. A women and her 2 children were taken to the hospital with non life threatening injuries.

Along with the heavy snow came very strong winds of 30 to 50 mph causing widespread blowing and drifting snow. Roads, highways, along with Interstates 29 and 90 were closed for a time. Schools started late or were closed on Monday the 11th.  A man died from exposure when he left his vehicle after he became stranded about three miles west of Redfield.  Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Murdo; 7 inches at Kennebec and Onida; 8 inches east of Hayes and Bowdle; 9 inches at Roscoe; 10 inches at Watertown, Miller, Clear Lake, Doland, and Highmore; and 11 inches at Castlewood.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Clark and Ipswich; 13 inches at Columbia and Aberdeen; 14 inches at Milbank and Faulkton; 15 inches at Waubay; 16 inches at Britton and Victor; 17 inches at Sisseton, Summit, and Wilmot; 18 inches at Webster; and 19 inches three miles west of Sisseton. The highest wind gust was 48 mph at Pierre during the late afternoon of the 10th. The snow began between 8 pm and Midnight on the 9th and ended in the late afternon of the 10th across central South Dakota and around noon on the 11th across the northeast.

An Arctic front dropping in behind an exiting low pressure area brought some scattered light snow showers, very strong winds of 30 to 50 mph, along with widespread blowing snow.  With the deep existing snow cover along with the new snowfall, ground blizzard conditions occurred from the late morning to the evening across much of northeast South Dakota.  Additional amounts from a trace up to an inch occurred. Frequent whiteout conditions brought extremely hazardous travel, with travel not advised along with several roads closed. Many mortorists were stranded and had to be rescued. Several schools were closed early and opened late on Tuesday the 19th. The highest wind gust was 47 mph at Hayti and Summit.  In part due to the low visibility and blowing snow, a van and a semi collided on U.S. Highway 281 two miles south of Warner around 11 am on the 18th. A women and her 2 children were taken to the hospital with non life threatening injuries.

A strong storm system developed across the Central Rockies and moved across the Central Plains. The track of the storm brought the heaviest snow across southern South Dakota. North to northwest wind gusts to 50 mph also developed as the system passed to the south. The combination of snow and wind produced blizzard conditions at times across southwestern into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of three to six inches were common across the area, with as much as eight inches across Tripp County. Upslope enhanced snowfall across the northern Black Hills produced six to 12 inches of accumulation.

Variable snowfall of 2 to 8 inches, northwest winds gusting to 45 mph, and snow cover existing before the storm in part of the area, produced blizzard conditions with visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow in many areas. The low visibilities and drifting snow forced some businesses to close, and also forced several school closings on Monday February 11th. The blizzard closed many roads, including Interstates 90 and 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 5 inches was accompanied by northwest winds gusting to 45 mph, producing blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities below a quarter mile. The low visibilities and drifting snow closed roads including Interstate 90, some businesses, and forced school closings on Monday February 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought widespread accumulating snowfall along with very strong northwest winds to northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 1 to 4 inches along with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to near 60 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted. Some businesses and schools were also closed. Some snowfall amounts included; 1 inch at Sisseton; 2 inches south of Bristol and at Bowdle; 3 inches near Big Stone City; and 4 inches at Summit.  The highest wind gust was 58 mph at Aberdeen and near Summit. The snowfall began between 5 and 7 pm on the 17th and ended between 5 and 9 pm on the 18th.

Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in March. Precipitation was roughly half of normal over most of the area, with drought rated severe to exceptional over all of the area, including the extreme southeast, where precipitation was only slightly below normal. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with below normal precipitation and runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme to exceptional drought abated slowly to the moderate to severe category over most of southeast South Dakota during April. Near to a little above normal precipitation was not enough to end the long standing drought entirely, given the long term dry soil conditions that carried over into the month. No new effects of the drought were noted during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of April abated a little during the month, with extreme to exceptional drought becoming severe to extreme. The improvement was due to normal to above normal precipitation, although low Missouri River flows tempered the improvement. No new effects were noted, but the area remained sensitive to possible worsening again of conditions, depending on precipitation during the planting and growing seasons.

Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in March. Precipitation was roughly half of normal over most of the area, with drought rated severe to exceptional over all of the area, including the extreme southeast, where precipitation was only slightly below normal. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with below normal precipitation and runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought widespread accumulating snowfall along with very strong northwest winds to northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 1 to 4 inches along with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to near 60 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted. Some businesses and schools were also closed. Some snowfall amounts included; 1 inch at Sisseton; 2 inches south of Bristol and at Bowdle; 3 inches near Big Stone City; and 4 inches at Summit.  The highest wind gust was 58 mph at Aberdeen and near Summit. The snowfall began between 5 and 7 pm on the 17th and ended between 5 and 9 pm on the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved northeast across the Central Plains, producing heavy snow across the region. The northern edge of the heavier snow slid across south central South Dakota during the morning and early afternoon. Precipitation started as a mix of rain, freezing rain, and sleet in some areas during the early morning. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches were reported, with the highest amounts across Tripp County. Gusty northwest winds also produced blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe to exceptional drought conditions continued across most of central and into a small part of northeast South Dakota throughout March.  The counties in exceptional drought were Stanley, Jones, and Lyman. Extreme drought covered Dewey, Sully, Potter, Hughes, Buffalo, and far southern Hand and Hyde counties. Walworth, Campbell, Corson, McPherson, Edmunds, and most of the rest of Hyde and Hand counties remained in severe drought through the end of the month. Also, most of Clark, Hamlin, and Deuel counties remained in severe drought throughout March. Calculated soil moisture deficits over central South Dakota continued to range from 2 to 4 inches. Winter wheat continued to generally be rated from very poor to fair across the region.  The fire danger had also been an issue throughout the month across the snow-free areas of central South Dakota with the dry fuels and off and on windy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced flash flooding and large hail in southeast South Dakota from late evening of May 25th into the morning of May 26th. While many of the storms were in the far eastern part of the state, including the Sioux Falls area, there were also scattered storms further west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of up to 5 inches in a short time caused widespread street flooding with water up above wheels in some places. There were also homes that has basement and lower floor flooding. The rain also caused flash flooding of sections of the Big Sioux River, including the Tuthill Park area in Sioux Falls. There was damage to homes, roads, and home possessions.

Drought conditions continued over all of southeast South Dakota in March. Precipitation was roughly half of normal over most of the area, with drought rated severe to exceptional over all of the area, including the extreme southeast, where precipitation was only slightly below normal. There continued to be a lack of soil moisture as winter approached its end, since even normal winter precipitation is low compared to the warmer seasons. Also, the frozen ground forced runoff of what precipitation and snowmelt there was. There was little noted in the way of new effects of the drought, with the dry conditions giving a poor outlook for the Spring and Summer, including the winter wheat crop, which was also affected by poor germination conditions in the dry fall. Water restrictions continued to be few because of the low water usage, but the area was deemed vulnerable to even marginally dry weather if it developed in the spring and summer. Some effect was noted on livestock, although the majority of the livestock was in good condition despite persistent feed shortages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions continued in March, with below normal precipitation and runoff over frozen ground leaving soil moisture conditions very dry. Few new effects were noted, though the dry conditions coming out of winter were expected to immediately affect the winter wheat crop, as they had affected germination in the fall. Seasonally low water usage resulted in few other new effects. The main problem, aside from winter livestock feed, was the poor outlook for the spring and summer, which were expected to be extremely sensitive to dry weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought widespread accumulating snowfall along with very strong northwest winds to northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 1 to 4 inches along with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to near 60 mph caused widespread blizzard conditions. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted. Some businesses and schools were also closed. Some snowfall amounts included; 1 inch at Sisseton; 2 inches south of Bristol and at Bowdle; 3 inches near Big Stone City; and 4 inches at Summit.  The highest wind gust was 58 mph at Aberdeen and near Summit. The snowfall began between 5 and 7 pm on the 17th and ended between 5 and 9 pm on the 18th.

A surface low pressure area moving across the region brought warm moist air overtop low level cold air which resulted in widespread freezing rain across much of northeast South Dakota during the evening and into the early morning hours of the 8th and 9th.  For much of the area, the precipitation changed over to all rain before ending as temperatures rose to above freezing. Ice accumulations ranged from a tenth of an inch to a half of an inch.  Some ice accumulation reports included; 0.25 inches at Garden City, Hayti, Waubay, Clear Lake, Sisseton, and Summit; and 0.50 inches near Florence and Clark. Travel became hazardous with no significant accidents reported.  Also, no problems with power outages or tree damage were reported.

Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain and freezing drizzle producing light to moderate ice accumulations, then changing to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 12 inches at White Lake. Several branches and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. The winter precipitation made travel very difficult to impossible, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area brought heavy snow to much of northern South Dakota.  Much of the precipitation began as a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow before changing to all snow.  Some ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch occurred with the freezing rain causing some slick roads. The heavy snow continued through the day ending in the early morning hours of the 15th. Travel was significantly affected or halted. ||Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Sisseton, Andover, and south of Bristol; 7 inches at Britton; 8 inches northwest of Britton, northeast of Isabel, Roy Lake, Selby, Stratford, and south of Keldron; 9 inches at Eureka, Mobridge, and southeast of McIntosh; 10 inches at Groton, Victor, and Mellette, and 11 inches at Aberdeen, Ipswich, Bath, and Bowdle.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Seneca and Artas; 13 inches at Leola and Roscoe; 14 inches at Onaka, Waubay, and Pollock; 15 inches at Elm Lake and Faulkton; 16 inches at Gettysburg and north of Hoven and 17 inches at McLaughlin.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and evening of June 21st. There were also two brief tornadoes reported. A large upper level low pressure area brought heavy snow to much of northern South Dakota.  Much of the precipitation began as a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow before changing to all snow.  Some ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch occurred with the freezing rain causing some slick roads. The heavy snow continued through the day ending in the early morning hours of the 15th. Travel was significantly affected or halted. ||Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Sisseton, Andover, and south of Bristol; 7 inches at Britton; 8 inches northwest of Britton, northeast of Isabel, Roy Lake, Selby, Stratford, and south of Keldron; 9 inches at Eureka, Mobridge, and southeast of McIntosh; 10 inches at Groton, Victor, and Mellette, and 11 inches at Aberdeen, Ipswich, Bath, and Bowdle.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Seneca and Artas; 13 inches at Leola and Roscoe; 14 inches at Onaka, Waubay, and Pollock; 15 inches at Elm Lake and Faulkton; 16 inches at Gettysburg and north of Hoven and 17 inches at McLaughlin.

Long term dry soil conditions and low flows on the Missouri River contributed to lingering drought at the start of May. The severe to extreme drought steadily abated as precipitation increased during the first part of the month, with the sensitivity of the area to potential dry summer periods decreasing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of May improved steadily in early May with increased precipitation, and the effect of low Missouri River flows also decreased. The sensitivity of crops and water supplies to future dry weather steadily decreased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Lawrence County and tracked northeast into southeastern Butte County before weakening. The storm produced hail to the size of quarters in the Spearfish and Saint Onge areas.

With the much above normal precipitation in the form of mainly snowfall across the region through the month of April, drought conditions continued to improve across all of central and northeast South Dakota. Most of the region improved by one category on their drought conditions. Moderate to severe drought conditions across northeast South Dakota went to mainly abnormally dry from the beginning to the end of April. The exceptional drought conditions in central South Dakota diminished to extreme drought throughout April. The severe and extreme drought areas also diminished across central and north central South Dakota throughout the month. Soil moisture conditions improved by the end of the month.

An intense and slow moving spring snow storm impacted the area for over 48 hours. This storm brought heavy snowfall to much of the area, especially from the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 12 to 24 inches were reported across much of the Black Hills and south central South Dakota plains, with some locations receiving around 30 inches. The Rapid City Regional Airport received a record 28.2 inches of snow, including a record 20 inches in one day. To the north and east of the Black Hills, six to 18 inches of snow fell. Snowfall tapered off near the North Dakota border, where less than six inches of snow fell. Wind gusts to 40 mph produced considerable blowing and drifting snow on the plains. Interstate 90 was closed across much of western South Dakota for 24 hours.

Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain and freezing drizzle producing light to moderate ice accumulations, then changing to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 10 inches near Mitchell. Several branches and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. The winter precipitation made travel very difficult to impossible, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

After a rather quiet start to May, the weather pattern turned unsettled and wet during the middle of the month.  A large upper level low pressure system spinning over the region for a few days brought much needed moderate to heavy rainfall from the 17th through the 22nd.  This caused most of the region to improve by one category on their drought conditions. The severe to extreme drought area along and west of the Missouri River at the beginning of the month improved by the middle of the month with only a small portion of southwest Corson county in extreme drought and the rest having moderate to severe drought conditions.  By the end of the month, only southwest Stanley and most of Jones County had severe drought.

A large slow moving upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought several rounds of heavy snow to much of central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 6 to as much as 22 inches occurred over the several day period. Travel became difficult if not impossible with some roads closed for a time.  Interstate-90 was closed the evening of the 9th.  Many schools were also closed across the region. Additionally, a 66 year old male suffered a heart attack and passed away while shoveling the snow in front of his house in Aberdeen. ||Snowfall amounts included; 6 inches south of Bristol, west of Eagle Butte, Doland, Elm Lake, Ipswich, Groton, Raymond, and Sisseton; 7 inches at Castlewood, Aberdeen, Kidder, and Stratford; 8 inches at Miller, Waubay, 5 miles west of Mission Ridge, Hayti, and Turton; 9 inches at Bath, Clark, Wilmot, and Gann Valley; 10 inches south of Harrold, Watertown, Milbank, and Mellette and 11 inches at Bryant and Murdo.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Summit, 14 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, and Kennebec; 16 inches at Ree Heights, 18 inches at Highmore and 22 inches east of Hayes.

A large upper level low pressure trough combined with warm air aloft and cold air at the surface to bring freezing rain to parts of northeast South Dakota. Ice accumulations of a quarter to a half inch occurred on power lines and trees. Some power poles and tree branches were also downed. The glaze of ice caused some power outages along with dangerous travel conditions. Power outages affected up to 2300 customers for several hours. The freezing rain then changed to snow with some heavy snow across the northern part of the region. Some ice accumulation amounts included; a quarter of an inch in Groton, Doland, Clark, Watertown, Estelline, and south of Gary.  Ice accumulations up to half inch occurred south of Garden City.

Heavy rain fell over the James River basin in North Dakota during the early portion of the month.  The added influx of water caused the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers to increase the releases of water from the Jamestown Reservoir and the Pipestem Reservoir.  The James River rose to just over flood stage to 13.2 feet by the end of the month and continued to rise into June. Low-lying agricultural land began to flood along the river.

Heavy rain falling over the Grand River basin caused the river to climb above the 15 foot flood stage during the early afternoon hours of the 31st. The highest flood stage reached was 15.80 feet on the 31st. The flooding mainly affected low lying pasture and hay land along the river. The river went quickly below flood stage in the early morning hours of June 1st.

Ice accumulations of a quarter to a half inch occurred on power lines and trees. Some power poles and tree branches were also downed. The glaze of ice caused some power outages along with dangerous travel conditions. Power outages affected up to 2300 customers for several hours. The freezing rain then changed to snow with some heavy snow across the northern part of the region. Some ice accumulation amounts included; a quarter of an inch in Groton, Doland, Clark, Watertown, Estelline, and south of Gary.  Ice accumulations up to half inch occurred south of Garden City.

Heavy precipitation with rapid cooling produced a combination of freezing rain, sleet, and snow over all of southeast South Dakota from the early morning hours of April 9th, to the morning of April 11th. Moderate to heavy ice accumulations were reported near Interstate 90. These were followed by moderate to heavy snow and sleet accumulations, mainly near and north of Interstate 90. The ice accumulations, greater than a half inch in some areas, caused major tree and power line damage in the ice storm area. Fallen branches and limbs were numerous, trees were destroyed, roads were blocked, and some vehicles and homes suffered damages from the falling trees, limbs, and branches. There were also major power outages because of the power line and power pole damage. Strong winds with and following the storm, not strong enough to cause significant damage under dry conditions, contributed greatly to the tree and power line damage. The snow and sleet accumulations made travel difficult, especially in areas where downed trees and power lines were already causing road blockages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing heavy ice accumulations, ranging up to more than a half inch in Sioux Falls. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 10 inches near Wall Lake. Very many branches, limbs, whole trees, and power lines were downed by the weight of ice and accompanying wind. Both the Sioux Falls area and other urban areas and rural electric cooperatives suffered major power line damage.|Trees and tree debris blocked many roads, and damaged several vehicles and homes. Major power outages were reported, affecting tens of thousands of people. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of precipitation began with freezing rain quickly producing heavy ice accumulations, especially in the northern part of the county, with more than a half inch in southern Sioux Falls. The precipitation then changed to sleet and then snow, with sleet and snow accumulations reaching 9 inches in southern Sioux Falls. The winter precipitation made travel impossible in many areas, resulting in schools and businesses being forced to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense and slow moving spring snow storm impacted the area for over 48 hours. This storm brought heavy snowfall to much of the area, especially from the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 12 to 24 inches were reported across much of the Black Hills and south central South Dakota plains, with some locations receiving around 30 inches. The Rapid City Regional Airport received a record 28.2 inches of snow, including a record 20 inches in one day. To the north and east of the Black Hills, six to 18 inches of snow fell. Snowfall tapered off near the North Dakota border, where less than six inches of snow fell. Wind gusts to 40 mph produced considerable blowing and drifting snow on the plains. Interstate 90 was closed across much of western South Dakota for 24 hours.

A large slow moving upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought several rounds of heavy snow to much of central and northeast South Dakota. After a rather quiet start to May, the weather pattern turned unsettled and wet during the middle of the month.  A large upper level low pressure system spinning over the region for a few days brought much needed moderate to heavy rainfall from the 17th through the 22nd.  This caused most of the region to improve by one category on their drought conditions. The severe to extreme drought area along and west of the Missouri River at the beginning of the month improved by the middle of the month with only a small portion of southwest Corson county in extreme drought and the rest having moderate to severe drought conditions.  By the end of the month, only southwest Stanley and most of Jones County had severe drought.

Hail, up to golf ball size, was reported mostly in north central South Dakota. Locally heavy rainfall occurred from southeast Sully County, across central Hyde and Hand County. Rainfall amounts included, 2.09 inches near Highmore; 2.30 inches 20 miles southeast of Onida; 2.90 inches 11 miles northwest of Miller; 3.63 inches 3 miles southeast of Miller; 4.10 inches near Wessington; and 4.50 inches 6 miles north of Miller.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail completely covered the ground.

Interstate 90 was closed across much of western South Dakota for 24 hours.

An intense and slow moving spring snow storm impacted the area for over 48 hours. This storm brought heavy snowfall to much of the area, especially from the Black Hills into south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts of 12 to 24 inches were reported across much of the Black Hills and south central South Dakota plains, with some locations receiving around 30 inches. A storm system moving east-southeast along a warm front interacted with increasing low level winds and an abnormally moist air-mass to result in severe storms across north central and central South Dakota. Hail, up to golf ball size, was reported mostly in north central South Dakota. Locally heavy rainfall occurred from southeast Sully County, across central Hyde and Hand County. Rainfall amounts included, 2.09 inches near Highmore; 2.30 inches 20 miles southeast of Onida; 2.90 inches 11 miles northwest of Miller; 3.63 inches 3 miles southeast of Miller; 4.10 inches near Wessington; and 4.50 inches 6 miles north of Miller.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and evening of June 21st. There were also two brief tornadoes reported. The majority of severe weather reports were from north of Interstate 90, but there were a few reports to the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought several rounds of heavy snow to much of central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 6 to as much as 22 inches occurred over the several day period. Travel became difficult if not impossible with some roads closed for a time.  Interstate-90 was closed the evening of the 9th.  Many schools were also closed across the region. Additionally, a 66 year old male suffered a heart attack and passed away while shoveling the snow in front of his house in Aberdeen. ||Snowfall amounts included; 6 inches south of Bristol, west of Eagle Butte, Doland, Elm Lake, Ipswich, Groton, Raymond, and Sisseton; 7 inches at Castlewood, Aberdeen, Kidder, and Stratford; 8 inches at Miller, Waubay, 5 miles west of Mission Ridge, Hayti, and Turton; 9 inches at Bath, Clark, Wilmot, and Gann Valley; 10 inches south of Harrold, Watertown, Milbank, and Mellette and 11 inches at Bryant and Murdo.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Summit, 14 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, and Kennebec; 16 inches at Ree Heights, 18 inches at Highmore and 22 inches east of Hayes.

A large upper level low pressure area brought heavy snow to much of northern South Dakota.  Much of the precipitation began as a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow before changing to all snow. Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Seneca and Artas; 13 inches at Leola and Roscoe; 14 inches at Onaka, Waubay, and Pollock; 15 inches at Elm Lake and Faulkton; 16 inches at Gettysburg and north of Hoven and 17 inches at McLaughlin.

A weak upper level disturbance moving across South Dakota interacted with an unstable surface environment resulting in severe thunderstorms. Winds gusting up to near 80 mph in Clark county caused some damage. However with a slow storm motion, some thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.  Some rainfall amounts included; 3.24 inches 10 miles southeast of Roscoe; 2.36 inches near Herreid; 2.19 inches in Roscoe; 2.17 inches near Hoven and 1.92 inches in Onida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was downed and there was partial damage to a warehouse roof northeast of town.

While golf ball size hail occurred, the main threat with these thunderstorms was the destructive winds in excess of 80 mph where they caused some damage from Presho and east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A camper and heating unit at the school was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding of the Vermillion River, with lowlands including some farmland flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor farmland and other lowland flooding of the Vermillion River. The river crested at 2.0 feet above flood stage near Davis on May 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions and low flows on the Missouri River contributed to lingering drought at the start of May. The severe to extreme drought steadily abated as precipitation increased during the first part of the month, with the sensitivity of the area to potential dry summer periods decreasing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of May improved steadily in early May with increased precipitation, and the effect of low Missouri River flows also decreased. The sensitivity of crops and water supplies to future dry weather steadily decreased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Lawrence County and tracked northeast into southeastern Butte County before weakening. The storm produced hail to the size of quarters in the Spearfish and Saint Onge areas.

A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Lawrence County and tracked northeast into southeastern Butte County before weakening. The storm produced hail to the size of quarters in the Spearfish and Saint Onge areas.

After a rather quiet start to May, the weather pattern turned unsettled and wet during the middle of the month.  A large upper level low pressure system spinning over the region for a few days brought much needed moderate to heavy rainfall from the 17th through the 22nd.  This caused most of the region to improve by one category on their drought conditions. The severe to extreme drought area along and west of the Missouri River at the beginning of the month improved by the middle of the month with only a small portion of southwest Corson county in extreme drought and the rest having moderate to severe drought conditions.  By the end of the month, only southwest Stanley and most of Jones County had severe drought.

Additionally, a 66 year old male suffered a heart attack and passed away while shoveling the snow in front of his house in Aberdeen. ||Snowfall amounts included; 6 inches south of Bristol, west of Eagle Butte, Doland, Elm Lake, Ipswich, Groton, Raymond, and Sisseton; 7 inches at Castlewood, Aberdeen, Kidder, and Stratford; 8 inches at Miller, Waubay, 5 miles west of Mission Ridge, Hayti, and Turton; 9 inches at Bath, Clark, Wilmot, and Gann Valley; 10 inches south of Harrold, Watertown, Milbank, and Mellette and 11 inches at Bryant and Murdo.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Summit, 14 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, and Kennebec; 16 inches at Ree Heights, 18 inches at Highmore and 22 inches east of Hayes.

A large upper level low pressure area brought heavy snow to much of northern South Dakota.  Much of the precipitation began as a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow before changing to all snow.  Some ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch occurred with the freezing rain causing some slick roads. The heavy snow continued through the day ending in the early morning hours of the 15th. Travel was significantly affected or halted. ||Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Sisseton, Andover, and south of Bristol; 7 inches at Britton; 8 inches northwest of Britton, northeast of Isabel, Roy Lake, Selby, Stratford, and south of Keldron; 9 inches at Eureka, Mobridge, and southeast of McIntosh; 10 inches at Groton, Victor, and Mellette, and 11 inches at Aberdeen, Ipswich, Bath, and Bowdle.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Seneca and Artas; 13 inches at Leola and Roscoe; 14 inches at Onaka, Waubay, and Pollock; 15 inches at Elm Lake and Faulkton; 16 inches at Gettysburg and north of Hoven and 17 inches at McLaughlin.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota during the afternoon and evening of June 21st. There were also two brief tornadoes reported. The majority of severe weather reports were from north of Interstate 90, but there were a few reports to the south.

Long term dry soil conditions and low flows on the Missouri River contributed to lingering drought at the start of May. The severe to extreme drought steadily abated as precipitation increased during the first part of the month, with the sensitivity of the area to potential dry summer periods decreasing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long term dry soil conditions at the start of May improved steadily in early May with increased precipitation, and the effect of low Missouri River flows also decreased. The sensitivity of crops and water supplies to future dry weather steadily decreased.

A storm system moving east-southeast along a warm front interacted with increasing low level winds and an abnormally moist air-mass to result in severe storms across north central and central South Dakota. Hail, up to golf ball size, was reported mostly in north central South Dakota. Locally heavy rainfall occurred from southeast Sully County, across central Hyde and Hand County. Rainfall amounts included, 2.09 inches near Highmore; 2.30 inches 20 miles southeast of Onida; 2.90 inches 11 miles northwest of Miller; 3.63 inches 3 miles southeast of Miller; 4.10 inches near Wessington; and 4.50 inches 6 miles north of Miller.

A large slow moving upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought several rounds of heavy snow to much of central and northeast South Dakota.  Snowfall amounts from 6 to as much as 22 inches occurred over the several day period. Travel became difficult if not impossible with some roads closed for a time.  Interstate-90 was closed the evening of the 9th.  Many schools were also closed across the region. Additionally, a 66 year old male suffered a heart attack and passed away while shoveling the snow in front of his house in Aberdeen. ||Snowfall amounts included; 6 inches south of Bristol, west of Eagle Butte, Doland, Elm Lake, Ipswich, Groton, Raymond, and Sisseton; 7 inches at Castlewood, Aberdeen, Kidder, and Stratford; 8 inches at Miller, Waubay, 5 miles west of Mission Ridge, Hayti, and Turton; 9 inches at Bath, Clark, Wilmot, and Gann Valley; 10 inches south of Harrold, Watertown, Milbank, and Mellette and 11 inches at Bryant and Murdo.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included; 12 inches at Summit, 14 inches at Onida, Clear Lake, and Kennebec; 16 inches at Ree Heights, 18 inches at Highmore and 22 inches east of Hayes.

An Arctic cold front and upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains, bringing heavy snow, gusty winds, and very cold temperatures. The heaviest snow fell across northwestern South Dakota, where six to 12 inches of accumulation was reported. Pockets of heavy snow were also reported over southwestern and south central South Dakota. Gusty winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities greatly at times.

Arctic air combined with northwest winds of 20 to 35 mph brought bitter cold wind chills to much of northeast South Dakota. Some of the wind chills include; 37 degrees below zero at Clear Lake; 38 degrees below zero at Sisseton and Watertown; and 43 degrees below zero at Hillhead in Marshall county.

Arctic air combined with northwest winds of 5 to 15 mph resulted in extreme wind chills of 35 below to 45 below zero across much of central and northeast South Dakota. Some of the wind chills include, 36 degrees below zero at Pierre and Eureka; 37 degrees below zero at Aberdeen; 38 degrees below zero at Faulkton and Sisseton; 40 degrees below zero at Watertown; 41 degrees below zero at Hayti and Webster, and 43 degrees below zero at Danforth.

Snow, heavy in some areas, and accompanied by moderate northerly winds, accumulated 3 to 8 inches in part of east central and far southeast South Dakota from the afternoon of November 5th, to just after midnight on November 6th. An Arctic cold front and upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains, bringing heavy snow, gusty winds, and very cold temperatures. The heaviest snow fell across northwestern South Dakota, where six to 12 inches of accumulation was reported. Pockets of heavy snow were also reported over southwestern and south central South Dakota. Gusty winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities greatly at times.

A surface low pressure system, along with an upper level trough of low pressure brought widespread severe weather across north central and northeastern South Dakota.  Severe storms produced large hail up to two inches in diameter, winds up to 70 mph, along with some isolated flash flooding.  These conditions caused damage to homes, vehicles, crops, and trees. Locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 5 inches in Spink County resulted in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of a gravel driveway was washed out requiring some repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic blizzard pounded western South Dakota with record-setting snowfall and strong winds for almost 48 hours from the evening of October 3 through the afternoon of October 5. One to two feet of snow was reported over the plains of western South Dakota, with three to five feet of snow falling over the northern and central Black Hills. Wind gusts to 70 mph across the plains produced significant blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities near zero for much of the day on October 4. The heavy wet snow and strong winds downed trees and power lines, causing prolonged outages and impassible highways. The roofs of several businesses, a middle school, and community center collapsed from the heavy snow. Thousands of livestock were killed from hypothermia, suffocation, or drowning. The South Dakota Animal Industry Board received reports of over 21,000 cattle; over 1300 sheep; 400 horses; and 40 bison deaths from the storm. Tree and debris removal costs were several million dollars.

A very strong low pressure area moving across the region brought an early fall blizzard to most of the counties west of Missouri River on October 4th and 5th. The snowfall and blizzard conditions occurred mainly along the western parts of the counties. The snowfall amounts varied largely from 1 to 2 inches to as much as 22 inches in far western Corson county. Very strong northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 60 mph brought blizzard conditions and significant travel problems. Interstate-90 was closed from Murdo to the Wyoming border from 7 pm on October 4th to the evening of the October 8th. No travel was advised on all roads west of the Missouri River. Many cattle were also lost in western Corson county due to the storm. The heavy snow along with strong winds resulted in the some power outages along with some downed tree branches. The snowfall began in the late morning hours of the 4th and ended in the early afternoon hours of the 5th.  Some snowfall amounts that occurred were 1 inch at Murdo; 2 inches at Timber Lake and 5 miles west of Hayes; 4 inches at McIntosh; 16 inches southeast of Morristown; and 22 inches southwest of Keldron.
